title: How to save energy in the kitchen
description: Practical ways to cut kitchen energy costs: kettle habits, fridge settings, dishwasher eco mode and a cost table comparing oven, air fryer and microwave.
blurb: Kettle habits, fridge settings and smarter appliance choices that trim £50 to £100 a year off kitchen energy costs.
lede: The kitchen is the hardest-working room in the house. It holds the only appliances that never switch off, the most power-hungry gadget you own, and a dozen daily habits that each cost a penny or two and quietly add up to serious money over a year. The good news is that kitchen savings are mostly free. You do not need a new oven or a smart gadget, just a clearer idea of what each job actually costs and a few small changes to how you boil, cook, chill and wash up. This guide puts real numbers on all of it at current prices.
short_answer: A typical household can cut £50 to £100 a year in the kitchen without spending anything: boil only the water you need, use the microwave or air fryer instead of the oven for small portions, keep lids on pans, run the dishwasher full on eco, and set the fridge to 3 to 5°C with clean door seals. At 26.11p per kWh (Ofgem cap, July to September 2026), every kilowatt hour you avoid is worth about 26p, so the wins come from habits you repeat hundreds of times a year.

sections.h2: Where the kitchen money actually goes
sections.html:
Kitchen energy use splits into three rough buckets. The first is cold storage: the fridge and freezer run 24 hours a day, every day, and a typical modern fridge freezer gets through 250 to 300 kWh a year, which is £65 to £78 at the current cap. An older or faulty one can use half as much again. The second is cooking: kettle, hob, oven, microwave and whatever counter-top gadgets you own. The third is washing up, where the dishwasher heats its own water electrically and a single standard cycle can cost more than an hour of television.

That split matters because the savings work differently in each bucket. Cold appliances reward one-off maintenance: set the right temperature, keep the seals clean, and the saving repeats itself silently for years. Cooking rewards habit changes, because you make dozens of small decisions a week about which appliance to switch on. Washing up rewards one settings change, eco mode, plus a bit of discipline about full loads. If you want the wider picture of what each device in your home costs to run, our electricity guides cover everything plug by plug.

sections.h2: Match the appliance to the job
sections.html:
The single biggest cooking saving is choosing the right tool for the portion in front of you. A full-size electric oven heats 50 to 70 litres of air, the metal around it and eventually your food. A microwave or air fryer puts most of its energy straight into the food. For one or two portions, the oven is usually the most expensive option on the worktop.

JobTypical energy usedCost per use
Kettle, one mug (300ml)0.035 kWh1p
Kettle, full 1.7 litres0.19 kWh5p
Microwave, 10 minute reheat0.2 kWh5p
Air fryer, 25 minutes0.5 kWh13p
Electric oven, 1 hour including preheat1.2 kWh31p
Electric oven, 2 hour casserole2.4 kWh63p
Slow cooker, 8 hours on low1.3 kWh34p
Gas hob, 30 minutes on a medium burner1.0 kWh (gas)7p

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Energy figures are typical mid-range appliances; check your own ratings plate.

Two things jump out of that table. First, swapping a one-hour oven session for 25 minutes in the air fryer saves about 18p a time, which is roughly £37 a year if you do it four times a week. Second, a slow cooker running all day costs about half what a two-hour oven casserole does, and far less than it feels like it should, because it only draws 150 to 200 watts. We compare the three head to head, with more dishes and timings, in our oven vs microwave vs air fryer guide.

When you do use the oven, make it earn its keep. Cook two or three things at once, skip preheating for anything that cooks longer than about 40 minutes, resist opening the door (each peek drops the temperature by 10 to 20°C), and switch off five to ten minutes early to let residual heat finish the job.

sections.h2: Kettle habits: the cheapest fix in the house
sections.html:
A kettle is the most powerful appliance most people own, typically 3kW, but it runs in short bursts so the cost per boil is small: about 1p for a single mug and 5p for a full 1.7 litre kettle. The waste comes from the gap between those two numbers. If you boil a full kettle four times a day to make one or two drinks, you are heating well over a litre of water nobody asked for.

Three habits fix it:

  • Measure with the mug. Fill the cup you are about to use, pour it into the kettle, boil that. Most kettles need a minimum of around 500ml to cover the element, so two mugs' worth is usually the practical floor.
  • Boil once, not twice. Reboiling a kettle that has gone cold costs the same as the first boil. If you routinely walk away and forget, fill less so there is less to reheat.
  • Descale it. In hard water areas a layer of limescale on the element slows the boil and wastes energy. A 30p citric acid soak every couple of months keeps it clean.

For a household that overfills for every round of tea, fixing this is worth roughly £10 to £15 a year. Small on its own, but it is the easiest saving in this entire guide and it sits alongside a dozen similar fixes in our quick wins under a tenner list.

sections.h2: The fridge and freezer: the only appliances that never sleep
sections.html:
Because cold appliances run constantly, small inefficiencies compound. A fridge freezer using 300 kWh a year costs about £78; a tired fifteen-year-old unit using 450 kWh costs about £117. You cannot switch them off, but you can stop them working harder than they need to:

  • Set the right temperatures. The fridge should be 3 to 5°C and the freezer minus 18°C. Every degree colder than necessary adds a few percent to consumption. Use a £2 fridge thermometer rather than trusting the dial, which is often just numbered 1 to 5.
  • Check the door seals. Close the door on a strip of paper; if it slides out easily, the seal is leaking cold air at that point. Clean the seals with warm soapy water, and replace them if they have hardened or split.
  • Give it breathing room. The coils at the back dump heat into the room. Leave a few centimetres of clearance, dust the coils once or twice a year, and avoid trapping the appliance next to the oven if your layout allows.
  • Cool food before it goes in. A hot pan of leftovers makes the compressor run hard and raises the temperature for everything around it. Let food steam off for 20 to 30 minutes first.
  • Defrost the freezer. More than about half a centimetre of frost acts as insulation in exactly the wrong place and makes the unit work harder.
  • Keep the freezer full, the fridge ventilated. A well-packed freezer holds its temperature better through door openings; even bottles of tap water filling dead space help. The fridge, by contrast, needs air circulating, so do not block the vents at the back.

If your unit is genuinely old and you are weighing up a replacement, the running-cost arithmetic and energy label details are in our fridge freezer efficiency guide.

On the hob, the physics is simple: heat that escapes the pan is money spent on warming the kitchen. Put lids on pans and you can cut the energy needed to keep something simmering by a half or more, because you stop paying to replace the heat lost as steam. Match the pan to the ring (a small pan on a big ring wastes heat around the sides), use just enough water to cover vegetables rather than drowning them, and turn the ring down once a pot reaches the boil; a furious boil cooks no faster than a gentle one, it just evaporates water more expensively.

Batch cooking multiplies every saving. Cooking four portions of a meal uses barely more energy than cooking one, and a 5p microwave reheat later in the week replaces an entire 31p oven session.

For washing up, the dishwasher is the rare appliance where the lazy option is also the cheap one, with two conditions. First, run it full: a half load uses almost the same energy as a full one. Second, use eco mode. It runs longer but heats the water less and uses less of it, and heating water is where nearly all the energy goes. A standard cycle at around 1.1 kWh costs about 29p; an eco cycle at around 0.8 kWh costs about 21p. At five loads a week, eco mode alone saves roughly £20 a year. The idea that a longer cycle must cost more is one of the most persistent misunderstandings in the kitchen, and it has company; our energy saving myths guide works through the others, including the old claim that hand-washing always beats the machine. Skip the heated dry option too: crack the door open at the end of the cycle and let the dishes air dry. The full settings walkthrough is in our dishwasher eco mode guide.

Kitchen savings are an accumulation of pennies, but they are pennies you spend thousands of times a year. Boil for the cups you need, reach for the microwave or air fryer before the oven for small portions, keep lids on pans, set the fridge to 3 to 5°C with clean seals, and run the dishwasher full on eco. None of it costs anything, none of it changes what you eat, and together it is realistically worth £50 to £100 a year for a typical household at current rates.

If you want to go further, the next tier is buying decisions rather than habits: a slow cooker if you cook stews and curries anyway, an air fryer if you currently fire up the oven for small jobs, and an efficient fridge freezer when the old one finally dies rather than before. Work each one out at your own unit rate before you buy; the per-use figures above give you the method. And once the kitchen is sorted, the same five-minute-fix mindset applies to the rest of the house, starting with our quick wins under a tenner.

faq.q: Is an air fryer really cheaper than an oven?
faq.a: For one or two portions, yes. A 25 minute air fryer session uses around 0.5 kWh (about 13p at 26.11p per kWh) against roughly 1.2 kWh (about 31p) for an hour of a full-size electric oven including preheat. The oven wins back ground when you cook for a crowd or fill several shelves at once, because its energy is spread across more food.

faq.q: Does dishwasher eco mode save money even though it takes longer?
faq.a: Yes. Eco mode heats the water to a lower temperature and uses less of it, which is where nearly all the energy goes; the extra time is just longer soaking and spraying with cold-running pumps. A typical eco cycle uses around 0.8 kWh against 1.1 kWh or more for a standard cycle, saving roughly 8p a load, or about £20 a year at five loads a week.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to boil water in a kettle or on the hob?
faq.a: On gas, the hob can work out slightly cheaper per litre because gas costs 7.33p per kWh against 26.11p for electricity, even though a gas flame wastes more heat around the pan. The difference is a penny or so per boil, and the kettle is faster and switches itself off. On an electric or induction hob, the kettle is the cheaper and quicker choice. Either way, the real saving is boiling only the water you need.

faq.q: What temperature should my fridge and freezer be set to?
faq.a: The fridge should be between 3 and 5°C and the freezer at minus 18°C. Colder settings waste energy without making food safer, and every unnecessary degree adds a few percent to running costs. Check with a cheap fridge thermometer rather than relying on the numbered dial.

faq.q: Do slow cookers use a lot of electricity?
faq.a: No. A slow cooker draws only 150 to 200 watts, so even 8 hours on low uses around 1.3 kWh, about 34p at current rates. That is roughly half the cost of running an electric oven for two hours to cook the same casserole, which makes it one of the cheapest ways to cook a hot meal.
